title: Archival notes
subtitle: A letter from Hannah Arendt to W.H. Auden
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

What is forgiveness? What is forgiveness in love?

In this letter to W. H. Auden written on February 14, 1960, Hannah Arendt reflects on forgiveness, distinguishing it from the Christian principle of charity and judicial pardon, and questions whether forgiveness can exist without repentance, before accepting a birthday invitation.

Until soon,
Sam

Share

Subscribe now

Become a member of Passages

Share

title: What's new
subtitle: Thank you
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

I hope you are enjoying spring!

Answers to some questions I’ve received about Passages

  • The Passages membership is separate from Substack. To say thank you for supporting my Substack, here is a 15% discount code for the yearly Passages membership: PASSAGES15 Enroll here.

  • Members do not need to sign-up for any of the courses or workshops I offer through Passages. They will be automatically enrolled. The membership is designed to make things easier for those who want to pay a one-time fee or quarterly fee and take anything they want. (Except for the writing workshops I’ll be offering.)

  • Everything I offer through my own platform, Passages, is pay-what-you-can. If you don’t want a membership, you can enroll through the stripe checkout page, for as little as $1.00. Click change price beneath the suggested amount or write to me for a link. I am committed to making these classes as accessible as possible.

  • Everyone who pays for a membership or a class covers the cost of those who can’t pay, allows me to pay the people I invite, covers the back-end, and supports my work. Thank you!

And thank you for your questions and supporting me! I am so grateful for your engagement.

Until soon,

Sam

Share

Join Passages

title: Personal ad from the archives
subtitle: Seeking relief from loneliness
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

This newspaper ad was taken out in 1913. The postmaster received a letter from a “country schoolmarm” in New Hampshire looking for correspondence companions to add some “interest” to her long summer vacation. Bachelors seeking relief from loneliness, open to receiving letters from a stranger, please apply.

Enjoy!

S

And join me for a six-week course this summer on loneliness!

Share

Passages Membership

The postmaster received a letter from a “country schoolmarm” in New Hampshire looking for correspondence companions to add some “interest” to her long summer vacation. Bachelors seeking relief from loneliness, open to receiving letters from a stranger, please apply.
And join me for a six-week course this summer on loneliness!

title: Sunday notes
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

I am typing with a cat in my lap. I hope you’re enjoying the spring weather. Brontë is learning how to spend time outdoors, though she seems quite skeptical of nature. I’m afraid she’s more of a 19th century bourgeois than an 18th century romantic.

Thank you for joining me on this journey into Passages. In case you missed it last week: courses and workshops are up below. (8 spots left in the writing workshop.) Watch out for a new workshop on the Art of Memory coming tomorrow. And join me for a deep dive into the ecstasies of language, loneliness, and solitude this summer.

Join Passages

This week a small newspaper clipping from 1913, offering a brief reflection on the relationship between immortality and loneliness.

And sad, lonely Beethoven:

Until soon,

Sam

Share

Subscribe now

Join Passages

title: The Art of Memory Workshop
subtitle: Techniques for memorization
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
The Art of Memory: How to memorize a poem

Memory is an art form. And one that seems to be in short supply these days. In this workshop we will learn about the art of memory as it was practiced by Cicero, St. Augustine, Thomas Aquinas, Dante, and C.S. Lewis. We will take a look at the rules outlined in the Ad Herrenium and other foundational texts on memory practices. We will then close with a step-by-step walk-through of how to use the “memory palace” technique to memorize Emily Dickinson’s “Hope is a thing with feathers” together.

When: Wednesday May 20, 2026

6:00pm EST to 7:30pm EST

Where: Live on zoom. Playback available after.

$45 (Suggested amount)

Enroll here*

If you are a member of passages you will be enrolled automatically.

*You can change the amount by clicking “change amount” beneath the suggested price.

This workshop will be led by memory coach and expert who runs the Craft of Memory Substack. Johnson is the host of the Craft of Memory Podcast, where he has interviewed 40+ memory experts and practitioners. He writes about the classical art of memory on Substack and specializes in verbatim text memorization and the use of mnemonics in language learning.

You can listen to our conversation here:

Subscribe now

Share

Become a member of Passages

title: Invitation to read The Waves
subtitle: May book: Virginia Woolf's The Waves
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

Join me for a three week course on Virginia Woolf’s The Waves.

The Waves is a work that will reaffirm your faith in life. It is what I reach for in moments of darkness to get lost in the ecstasy of language and turn over those questions from time immemorial of what it means to be alive.

Over the course of three weeks we will read The Waves together. It was Woolf’s most daring and experimental novel, and I hazard, her most triumphant. Capturing existence itself in modernity, she sets aside traditional narrative form and plot to draw us into being-in-language, cutting through the skin of appearances. A classic example of high-modernist fiction, Woolf wanted to capture through language “the exact shapes” the brain holds. Here, for her, there is no quarrel between the poets and the philosophers. Through soliloquies and lyrical passages, The Waves follows the lives and friendships of Bernard, Jinny, Susan, Rhoda, Louis, and Neville from childhood through adulthood, exploring consciousness itself in voice. And there is Percival who appears through perception alone, the one who has been lost. How are we to understand these “characters?” What makes this a work of high modernism? Can Woolf’s poetic reveries help us understand our world now? (For me the answer is yes.)

What You Get

  • Three

  • Live classes

  • Playbacks for those enrolled

  • Handouts

  • Access to private discussion board

Course Schedule

Sundays, 12pm - 1:30pm EST
May 3 — May 17, 2026
3 weeks
$225.00 (suggested amount)
Enroll here.

(If you’re a member no need to enroll. You will be sent the class information and playbacks automatically. Syllabus will be sent one week before we begin.)

Become a member of Passages

Share

title: Dante's Purgatorio
subtitle: Dante's Purgatorio: Poet, Prophet, Political Visionary with Elias Crim
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

Of the three realms in Dante’s Divine Comedy, the Purgatorio stands apart as the most deeply human. It is where Dante the poet comes alive, conversing with fellow artists, wrestling with questions of creativity, while exploring what it means to make art in a fallen world. Unlike the frozen fates of Hell or the eternal bliss of Paradise, Purgatory unfolds in real time, a place where hope is possible and souls can still change.

The politics here are earthly and urgent. Where the Inferno kept its gaze largely fixed on Florence, the Purgatorio lifts its eyes to all of Italy, painting it as a fractured kingdom desperate for moral and civic renewal, in order to inspire virtue and damn corruption.

Edition we’re using

For our common reference, I recommend we use the well-received translation by Robert and Jean Hollander. It has extensive notes and includes the Italian text. The translation is both accurate and readable.

If you’d like to explore versions which bring out more of the poetry, you might look those by at W.S. Merwin, D.M. Black or Jason Baxter.

In our class discussions, I will make a point to always refer to the line numbers of the Italian text so that if you prefer another version over the Hollander, that should work fine.

What You Get

  • Ten Live classes

  • Playbacks for those enrolled

  • Handouts

  • Access to private discussion board

Course Schedule

Tuesdays, 2:00pm - 3:30pm EST
May 5 — July 14, 2026
10 weeks
$350
Enroll here.

(If you’re a member no need to enroll. You will be sent the class information and playbacks automatically. Syllabus will be sent one week before we begin.)

Become a member of Passages

Reading Schedule

May 5

Canto 1: Morning; Venus; the four stars; Cato; the rush and the dew

Canto 2: The ship of souls; the angel pilot; Casella’s song; Cato’s rebuke

Canto 3: Dante’s shadow; the Contumacious; Manfred

May 12

Canto 4: The ascent; the sun’s course; Belacqua; the Lethargic

Canto 5: The Penitents of the last hour; Jacopo del Cassero; Buonconte da Montefeltro; La Pia

Canto 6: The power of intercession; Sordello; the disorders of Italy and Florence

May 19

Canto 7: The greetings of Virgil and Sordello; the Valley of the Princes

Canto 8: The guardian angels; Nino Visconti; the three stars; the serpent; Conrad Malaspina

Canto 9: The dream of the eagle; St. Lucy; the gate of Purgatory

May 26

Canto 10: The First Terrace; the sculptured wall; examples of humility; the purgation of pride

Canto 11: The Lord’s Prayer; Omberto Aldobrandeschi; Oderisi; Provenzan Salvani

Canto 12: The figured pavement; the proud brought low; the angel of humility

June 2

Canto 13: The Second Terrace; examples of kindness; the purgation of envy; Sapia

Canto 14: Guido del Duca; the Arno; the degeneracy of Romagna; examples of envy

Canto 15: The angel of mercy; spiritual partnership; the Third Terrace; visions of gentleness

June 9

Canto 16: The purgation of anger; Marco Lombardo; human degeneracy; the Church’s misguiding of the world

Canto 17: Visions of anger; the angel of peace; disordered love as the principle of sin

Canto 18: The exposition of love; the Fourth Terrace; the purgation of sloth: examples of zeal and sloth

June 16

Canto 19: The dream of the Siren; the angel of zeal; the Fifth Terrace; the purgation of avarice and prodigality; Pope Adrian V

Canto 20: Examples of generosity; Hugh Capet and the Capetian Dynasty; examples of avarice; the earthquake and the Gloria in Excelsis

Canto 21: Statius; the completion of his penance; his greeting of Virgil

June 23

Canto 22: Statius’ indebtedness to Virgil and his conversion; the Sixth Terrace; the purgation of gluttony; examples of temperance

Canto 23: The wasted form of the penitents; Forese Donati; his warning to the women of Florence

Canto 24: Bonagiunta; “the sweet new style”; the second tree; examples of gluttony; the angel of temperance

June 30

Canto 25: The generation of the body and creation of the soul; the Seventh Terrace; the purgation of lust; examples of chastity

Canto 26: Dante’s shadow on the flames; examples of lust; Guinicelli; Arnaut

Canto 27: The angel of chastity; the passage through the fire; the dream of Leah; Virgil’s last speech

July 7

Canto 28: The Earthly Paradise; the fair lady and the stream; the seeds dispersed on the earth

Canto 29: The walk by the river; the pageant of revelation

Canto 30: Beatrice on the chariot; the disappearance of Virgil; Beatrice’s rebuke of Dante

July 14

Canto 31: Dante’s confession; the passage through Lethe; Beatrice unveiled

Canto 32: The wheeling of the pageant; the great tree; disasters to the chariot

Canto 33: Beatrice’s promise of a deliverer; the sanctity of the tree; the passage through Eunoe

Share

Subscribe now

The politics here are earthly and urgent. Where theInfernokept its gaze largely fixed on Florence, thePurgatoriolifts its eyes to all of Italy, painting it as a fractured kingdom desperate for moral and civic renewal, in order to inspire virtue and damn corruption.

title: Welcome to Passages
subtitle: Courses, Workshops, Art, Literature, Poetry, Philosophy
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

For the past several years I have been teaching across three to five different public venues at a time. What I noticed is that the same faces kept appearing regardless of where I was teaching. So, I started offering classes here, with a suggested price and pay-what-you-can option. I did this because I believe in making literature, theory, philosophy, psychology, poetry, the humanities, as available as possible. And selfishly because I love to share what I love. I teach courses on what excites me in the hopes that you might find something exciting, too. And I teach the bibliography of what I am writing, interested to open up texts in new ways together. Expanding what I alone can do this past year, it’s been a pleasure to teach with and descend into Dante’s Inferno with .

The incredible part of this adventure for me is the feeling of community it has forged. In addition to allowing me to connect with readers, I feel we have an ongoing conversation, a place to think together.

So here is what I have done:

A little history

I launched this Substack as Illuminations during the pandemic to bring a little light in dark times. As we moved out of the pandemic, I rebranded it as Reflections to share a bit of poetry and music and philosophy with you as I began working on my new loneliness project. And now, keeping with my Walter Benjamin themes, and eyes on future aspirations, in its third incarnation I’m calling it: Passages.

Why Passages?

Passages in the literal textual sense that we will be reading many passages together. Close-reading might save the world, I think when I read Virginia Woolf, or Emily Dickinson, or Louise Glück. And passages in the sense that this is a new journey, a new pilgrimage for us to take together into a world of incredible writing and thinking to nourish the spirit, connection, and friendship. And passages as that threshold space between. It is that space in-between where interest, or inter-est as Arendt always fashioned it, may grow.

Spring and Summer Courses and Workshops

Thank you to everyone who filled out the survey. In order of desire: Novels, Loneliness, and Writing Workshop were the most requested classes. So, I’ve put together a spring and summer syllabus for you composed of three classes that revolve around loneliness and solitude. We will begin with Virginia Woolf’s The Waves, then spend six weeks in the literature of loneliness, before reading Henry David Thoreau’s Walden. I’ve also created a small (12 seats only) intensive writing workshop designed for everyone who is curious about what it means to write for a general public audience. In addition to the courses I am teaching, will also continue teaching Dante. And more coming…

Pricing

Many people said they preferred to pay per class, so I am going to keep pricing structured that way. And many said they would prefer to pay one price up front and take anything they wanted over the course of a year, so I’ve created an annual membership for those who prefer to enroll in everything all at once. If you choose to become a member, you will automatically have access to every class and workshop (minus the writing workshop), and receive all of the materials and video playbacks. I’ve created two options:

  • $1199/year

  • $333/3 months

Or

  • Enroll in The Waves

  • Enroll in Loneliness

  • Enroll in Thoreau’s Walden

As always, if price is a barrier to any class you want to take, just send me a note and I will send you a pay-what-you-can registration link so you can enroll in the course.

I will send invitations for fall courses in August: Hannah Arendt, Walter Benjamin, Ralph Waldo Emerson, Emily Dickinson, Georges Bataille, Maurice Blanchot, and others to name a few. Friendship, storytelling, empathy, evil, eros, desire, archives …

And I reserve the right to add a few surprises along the way.

I’m excited for this next chapter with you.

Until soon!

Sam

Share

Subscribe now

Become a member of Passages

title: Thoreau’s Walden
subtitle: Invitation to read Henry David Thoreau’s Walden
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

Let’s get lost in the woods with Thoreau and read Walden.

Over the course of four weeks we will read Walden; or, Life in the Woods together. What does it mean to retreat from society in order to live a deliberate life of attention set against the pressures of modern society? Thoreau retired to a cabin in the woods, far away enough from the noise, and close enough to the world, in order to think about those fundamental questions of life concerning solitude, economy, and how one ought to live. How might Thoreau’s work help us think about the noise of our world today? And might he help us find some peace inside it?

What You Get

  • Live classes

  • Playbacks for those enrolled

  • Handouts

  • Access to private discussion board

Course Schedule

Sundays, 12pm - 1:30pm EST
July 5 — July 26, 2026
4 weeks
$275
Enroll here.

(If you’re a member no need to enroll. You will be sent the class information and playbacks automatically. Syllabus will be sent one week before we begin.)

Become a member of Passages

Share

Over the course of four weeks we will readWalden; or, Life in the Woodstogether. What does it mean to retreat from society in order to live a deliberate life of attention set against the pressures of modern society? Thoreau retired to a cabin in the woods, far away enough from the noise, and close enough to the world, in order to think about those fundamental questions of life concerning solitude, economy, and how one ought to live. How might Thoreau’s work help us think about the noise of our world today? And might he help us find some peace inside it?
(If you’re a member no need to enroll. You will be sent the class information and playbacks automatically. Syllabus will be sent one week before we begin.)

title: The Waves
subtitle: Invitation to read Virginia Woolf's The Waves
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

Join me for a close reading of Virginia Woolf’s The Waves. The Waves is a work that will reaffirm your faith in life. It is what I reach for in moments of darkness to get lost in the ecstasy of language and turn over those questions from time immemorial of what it means to be alive.

Over the course of three weeks we will read The Waves together. It was Woolf’s most daring and experimental novel, and I hazard, her most triumphant. Capturing existence itself in modernity, she sets aside traditional narrative form and plot to draw us into being-in-language, cutting through the skin of appearances. A classic example of high-modernist fiction, Woolf wanted to capture through language “the exact shapes” the brain holds. Here, for her, there is no quarrel between the poets and the philosophers. Through soliloquies and lyrical passages, The Waves follows the lives and friendships of Bernard, Jinny, Susan, Rhoda, Louis, and Neville from childhood through adulthood, exploring consciousness itself in voice. And there is Percival who appears through perception alone, the one who has been lost. How are we to understand these “characters?” What makes this a work of high modernism? Can Woolf’s poetic reveries help us understand our world now? (For me the answer is yes.)

What You Get

  • Three

  • Live classes

  • Playbacks for those enrolled

  • Handouts

  • Access to private discussion board

Course Schedule

Sundays, 12pm - 1:30pm EST
May 3 — May 17, 2026
3 weeks
$225.00
Enroll here.

(If you’re a member no need to enroll. You will be sent the class information and playbacks automatically. Syllabus will be sent one week before we begin.)

title: Loneliness: Literature, Philosophy, Poetry, Theory
subtitle: A 6-week course to explore loneliness together
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

What is loneliness?

In this six week course, we will take a look at a different approaches to understanding loneliness.

Loneliness: Literature, Philosophy, Poetry, Theory

Across literature, philosophy, poetry, art, and theory, we will encounter loneliness as a disease, a friend, a ghost, a room, lost love, grief, and the three a.m. sound of the faucet dripping. Each week we will take a look at a specific essay, article, chapter, story, or poem that approaches loneliness from a different angle, including work from D.W. Winnicott, Frieda Fromm-Reichmann, Paul Tillich, Melanie Klein, Emily Dickinson, Maya Angelou, Anne Carson, and Thomas Wolfe, among others.

What You Get

  • Six live classes

  • Playbacks for those enrolled

  • Handouts

  • Access to private discussion board

Course Schedule

Sundays, 12:00pm - 1:30pm EST
May 24 — June 28, 2026

title: Writing Workshop
subtitle: Writing for a Public Audience
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Writing Workshop: Writing for a Public Audience

Who is this workshop for?

This five-week generative writing workshop is for everyone who is curious about what it means to write for a general public audience. Together, we will examine observation and thinking patterns, writing styles, and techniques in order to write effectively beyond the academia. How do you write a manuscript proposal? How do you pitch an editor? How do you translate your years of research into something people want to read? This workshop is designed to be fun and challenging, to help you get out of your head and into your voice. All you need is your creative desire and curiosity.

What You’ll Learn over 5 weeks

We will meet weekly on zoom for 2.5 hours to examine and explore what makes an effective piece of public writing. Every student will engage in writing exercises during class designed specifically to practice writing for a public audience. We will explore syntax, sound, rhetorical devices, the opening, middle, and end game of a good essay. You will learn how to identify structure in essays, articles, and books. Most of our time will be spent on interactive practices that draw on different writing techniques designed to help you find your unique voice as a writer.

Beginning with an idea, chapter, essay draft, you will experiment with prompts that help you develop your work and prepare it for submission or perhaps even publication. You will have an opportunity to talk with people in the public humanities who have been on this journey, and together we will demystify what it means to write for a public audience.

Week 1: Writing up

  • Clearing common obstacles to writing for a public audience

Week 2: What language can do

  • Getting out of your head and into your voice

Week 3: Playing with form

  • Approaches to style. Is it an essay, article, a book or a book chapter?

Week 4: Strategies for editing

  • Sculpting, de-tangling, texture, color

Week 5: Letting go

  • Pitches, proposals, agents, publicists, and the ups and downs of using social media

What You Get

  • Five interactive Zoom workshops

  • Opportunity to workshop writing with the class

  • Curated reading list and weekly exercises

  • Access to private discussion board

Course Schedule

Sundays, 3:00pm - 5:30pm EST
May 10 — June 7, 2026
$695

Enroll here.

Enrollment is capped at 12 students. I have two partially subsidized spots available. Write to me for information, no explanation needed. First come. After you enroll you will receive an email from me welcoming you to the workshop. One week before we begin you will receive a course packet with readings, exercises, and assignments.

Until soon,

Sam

Share

Subscribe now

title: Sunday notes
subtitle: March come what may
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

Happy March!

A house note: There’s some spots left in my course, Vienna Secession: Art and Revolt, which begins today at the Brooklyn Institute. You can register here.

March is the cruelest month, I’ve always thought. Between winter and spring. Thaw. Mud. A sense of imminence. It will appear. It will come. It’s coming. Does anyone else dislike winter as much as I do?

Ada Limón

Some Erik Satie:

Until soon…

Sam

Share

Subscribe now

title: Sunday notes
author: samantharosehill
raw_article:
Dear Reader,

The light has shifted. It’s warmer now. No need to make as many fires. This morning a butterfly visited me while I was writing. The shock of his fluttering orange polka dot wings made me look up from loneliness and seemed to signal the transition from the darkened interiors of winter’s gloom to the lightness of spring in all of its movement.

I took his flight as an invitation to follow. So, I closed my computer and went for a long walk through the countryside, down curious lanes lined with plane trees, across never-ending marsh fields, looking for a new path. Collecting the color pallet of these early days, all green, yellow, husk. Below some: Lichen, bellis perennis, coltsfoot, cardamines, the spent head, or pappus remnants of the involucre from the dandelion I made a wish on.

And many butterflies between, too.

Some notes for your Sunday.

Mary Oliver, Morning Poem

Every morning
the world
is created.
Under the orange
 
sticks of the sun
the heaped
ashes of the night
turn into leaves again
 
and fasten themselves to the high branches—
and the ponds appear
like black cloth
on which are painted islands
 
of summer lilies.
If it is your nature
to be happy
you will swim away along the soft trails
 
for hours, your imagination
alighting everywhere.
And if your spirit
carries within it
 
the thorn
that is heavier than lead—
if it’s all you can do
to keep on trudging—
 
there is still
somewhere deep within you
a beast shouting that the earth
is exactly what it wanted—
 
each pond with its blazing lilies
is a prayer heard and answered
lavishly,
every morning,
 
whether or not
you have ever dared to be happy,
whether or not
you have ever dared to pray.

Some Seamus Heaney:

Pink Moon mood

Until soon,
Sam

Share

Subscribe now

I took his flight as an invitation to follow. So, I closed my computer and went for a long walk through the countryside, down curious lanes lined with plane trees, across never-ending marsh fields, looking for a new path. Collecting the color pallet of these early days, all green, yellow, husk. Below some: Lichen, bellis perennis, coltsfoot, cardamines, the spent head, or pappus remnants of the involucre from the dandelion I made a wish on.
